reasoning: The 2020 IMO regulations reducing the sulphur limit from 3.5% to 0.5% were enacted primarily for severe public health and environmental reasons, projected to prevent over 570,000 premature deaths between 2020 and 2025 by reducing toxic air pollution and acid rain. While scientists note that this reduction effectively acted as an 'inadvertent marine cloud brightening termination shock' that has accelerated local ocean warming, returning to burning high-sulphur fuel is almost universally rejected. Instead, scientists advocating for marine cloud brightening propose using benign seawater (salt spray) to achieve the cooling effect without the catastrophic health consequences and acid rain caused by sulphur dioxide. Extensive searches show absolutely no political momentum, proposals at IMO/MEPC meetings, or serious academic advocacy for reversing the IMO limit back to 3.5%. The IMO remains intensely focused on reducing overall emissions (like greenhouse gases) to net-zero by near-2050, making a reversal to heavier sulphur pollution historically and politically unprecedented. Because the fundamental drivers against sulphur (immediate human mortality and acidification) heavily outweigh its geoengineering side-effect (which can be replicated safely with salt), the likelihood of the IMO bringing the limit back to 3.5% or greater is extremely close to zero. The lowest allowable probability is assigned to the immediate future, with very marginal increases for distant time horizons reflecting generalized temporal uncertainty.
